summary: Gruber revisits his earlier characterization of the native Mac OS X port of OpenOffice as vaporware, defending the label after the project announced a 2006 target date. He critiques both the open source community's outrage at the term and Andrew Orlowski's Register column wondering why more developers don't volunteer for the project. Gruber argues the answer is obvious: creating great Mac software is hard work that deserves compensation. He further argues that Apple has no incentive to staff an open source cross-platform office suite, preferring to build its own Mac-native software like Keynote and Safari.
key_points: The native Mac OS X port of OpenOffice fits the dictionary definition of vaporware, regardless of community objections to the label
The lack of volunteer developers is not a mystery — creating excellent user experiences is hard work that people reasonably expect to be paid for
Apple's strategic interest lies in building Mac-native software it owns, not contributing to cross-platform open source projects
Apple's pattern with Safari and Keynote shows it will leverage open source foundations but build proprietary products on top
Volunteer labor is unlikely to produce a top-notch office suite to rival Microsoft Office on Mac
money_quotes: Open source vaporware is no better than commercial vaporware.
Creating a top-notch user experience is extremely hard work, and it takes a lot of time.

Wondering why your friendly neighborhood Mac developer doesn't devote his nights and weekends to volunteering for OpenOffice is like wondering why your friendly neighborhood barber doesn't spend his spare time giving free haircuts.
How about the upstream benefit of getting paid for their time?
Apple's business is making Macintosh computers, and writing Macintosh software.
If they respond by assigning Apple developers to write office software, it's going to be Mac software, designed and owned by Apple.
I wouldn't hold my breath waiting for it to be produced by volunteer labor.
key_insight: Great software requires real investment, and neither volunteer labor nor cross-platform open source projects are likely to produce the kind of polished Mac-native experience that Apple or its users actually want.
